properties.scope: reference, interest (created); person, project, event, place, item (linked from)
properties.trigger: notes and remembering: save or note this, keep pasted text, remember something word-for-word, daily notes and journaling, clipping links or web pages, finding or quoting an earlier note, what did I write about X, reviewing or tidying the notebook

data.short_description: Second Brain mini-app: a personal notebook with verbatim recall. Notes are stored exactly as written (a paste comes back byte-for-byte, never paraphrased), quick thoughts land in a daily note, notes link to the people and projects they're about, and recall runs exact, then graph, then semantic. Full operating rules are in this entity's lore chunks: Data Model: Notes & Verbatim Capture; Data Model: Daily Notes, Links & the Note Index; Workflow: Capture a Note; Workflow: Recall — Word for Word; Workflow: Review & Tidy. Read all lore before acting on note requests.

lore.title: Data Model: Notes & Verbatim Capture
lore.content:
NOTE = reference entity. Name: a short, stable title — the user's title when they give one, otherwise the first line or a plain descriptive phrase ("API Design Notes", "Sourdough Hydration Table"). Slugs derive from names; once set, keep the name stable.

THE VERBATIM LAW: the user's words are stored EXACTLY as given — byte for byte. Pasted text, dictated thoughts, quotes: keep their markdown, indentation, blank lines, code, typos, and phrasing untouched. Never correct spelling, reformat, translate, tidy whitespace, or compress. If a summary or added structure would help, it goes in its own clearly labeled chunk ("Summary (assistant)") — the user's text is never edited to make room. Storage is faithful; polish is a separate, labeled layer.

Lore layout: the note body lives in a lore chunk titled "Note". Content over the 10,000-character chunk limit is split into parts titled "Note (1/3)", "Note (2/3)", "Note (3/3)" with sequential display_index. SPLITTING IS PURE SLICING: cut at paragraph boundaries where possible (a single paragraph or code block over the limit is cut mid-paragraph), every byte — including the blank lines at each cut — lands in exactly one part, and reassembly is concatenation in display_index order with nothing added or removed. Text is never reworded to fit.

ADDITIONS ARE NEW CHUNKS: to add to an existing note, create a new chunk titled "Note — added <YYYY-MM-DD>" (create_entity_lore) instead of rewriting the body — re-emitting stored text is exactly where careless editing corrupts it. Rewriting an existing chunk (update_entity_lore) is reserved for the few operations the workflows sanction, and LORE UPDATES REPLACE THE WHOLE CHUNK: read the current text first (get_entity_lore) and write the complete revision.

Properties:
- source_type: "note" (the property other apps type references with; a clipping stays "note" — its url marks the origin)
- captured: ISO date of first capture ("2026-07-15")
- url: for web clippings, the source URL
- source: one line on where the text came from when it isn't the user's own words ("pasted from the team wiki", "dictated in the car")
- status: omit normally; "superseded" when a newer note replaces it (the successor is named in a final lore line)

Tags (exact spellings; tags are case-sensitive): every note gets "second-brain"; daily notes also get "daily-note"; topic tags come from the Note Index (next chunk). TAG EDITS REPLACE THE WHOLE ARRAY: when retagging, read current tags first (get_entities), then write the complete new set, keeping every tag already there.

RETRIEVAL IS BY TAG: "second-brain" finds every note (search_entities with tags). interest/second_brain is the app's hub: its lore holds the Note Index; notes do not link to the hub.

lore.title: Data Model: Daily Notes, Links & the Note Index
lore.content:
DAILY NOTE = the capture inbox: one reference entity per day, created on the day's first quick capture, never in advance. Name exactly "Daily Note YYYY-MM-DD" in the user's local date ("Daily Note 2026-07-15" → reference/daily_note_2026_07_15) — the deterministic name is the lookup key for time-based recall. Days run midnight to midnight in the user's local time; when the local date isn't known, ask rather than guess. source_type "note", captured set, tags ["second-brain", "daily-note"].

Quick captures append as separate lore chunks, one chunk per capture, titled "HH:MM — <label>" ("09:42 — standup follow-up"; the user's local time — omit HH:MM rather than guess it), body verbatim. A paste too large for one chunk skips the inbox and goes straight to its own note (splitting is defined there), leaving a pointer capture behind. Never merge captures into one chunk: separate chunks make appending safe (no read-modify-write risk) and keep each capture individually findable by search_lore.

PROMOTION: when a capture outgrows the inbox — it has a lasting subject, or the user keeps returning to it — create a proper note entity carrying the verbatim text, then rewrite the daily chunk as a one-line pointer ("→ promoted to reference/api_design_notes"). The text moves byte-for-byte: create the note, read the new chunk back (get_entity_lore) and confirm it matches the capture exactly, and only then rewrite the pointer. Promotion and merge (see Review & Tidy) are the only times captured text is ever relocated — and never before the copy is verified. Offer promotion; don't force it.

LINKS: attach a note to the tracked entities it is about — person/project/event/place/item REFERENCES note, the same edge other apps use to attach documents. "What notes touch the kitchen remodel?" is then query_relationships on the project. Cross-references between notes ("see also") use REFERENCES note → note; if the world rejects a reference-sourced edge (get_world_context lists what's allowed), record a "See also: reference/<slug>" line in the note's lore instead. Create edges for the real subjects of a note, not every passing mention — two or three edges beat ten.

NOTE INDEX = lore chunk titled exactly "Note Index" on interest/second_brain: the controlled vocabulary of topic tags, one line each ("sourdough — baking experiments, starters, and links"). Before inventing a topic tag, read the index and reuse a fit; add a line when a genuinely new topic appears (read the current chunk first — LORE UPDATES REPLACE THE WHOLE CHUNK). The index is the map of the notebook: small, current, and read at the start of any capture or recall that involves topics.

lore.title: Workflow: Capture a Note
lore.content:
Triggered by save intent: "note this", "save this", "remember this exactly", "clip this", a paste followed by "keep this somewhere".

1. Choose the shelf: a quick, untitled thought → today's daily note (create it if this is the day's first capture). A titled or lasting subject → its own note entity. When unsure, capture to the daily note and say so — capture must never stall on questions.
2. Search before creating (search_entities by likely title words and the second-brain tag): if a note on the subject exists, offer adding to it (a new "Note — added <YYYY-MM-DD>" chunk — never a rewrite of the body) vs. a separate note. Never create a second note with a near-identical title.
3. Store per the Data Model: verbatim lore, captured date, url/source when applicable.
4. Tag: "second-brain" plus topic tags from the Note Index. A new topic tag needs no permission but must be recorded in the index in the same breath.
5. Link the subjects: REFERENCES edges from the tracked entities the note is about.
6. CONFIRM WHAT WAS WRITTEN, every time: entity name, tags, chunk count, word count, and links ("Saved 'API Design Notes' — 1 chunk, 312 words, tags second-brain + work; linked from project/platform_redesign."). A capture the user can't see is a note they'll never trust. Report skips and splits honestly.
7. NO AMBIENT CAPTURE: the app saves when asked or when intent is unmistakable — it never quietly hoovers conversation into notes. When something in passing seems worth keeping, offer to save it.

Interview the paste, don't edit it: if text arrives with no title or topic, ask for or propose one — but the body is already final.

lore.title: Workflow: Recall — Word for Word
lore.content:
THE RECALL LAW: when the user asks for a note back, re-read it (get_entity_lore) and reproduce the stored content EXACTLY — never from conversational memory, never summarized unless a summary was asked for. Multi-part notes are reassembled by concatenating their parts in display_index order with nothing added or removed. Verbatim playback covers every user-text chunk — "Note" and its parts, "Note — added …" additions, "Note (merged from …)" chunks — in display_index order; assistant-labeled chunks ("Summary (assistant)") are read out only on request. When whitespace or formatting matters (code, tables, anything they'll paste onward), return the text in a fenced block so nothing renders away. Any commentary stays clearly separated from the quoted text.

The retrieval ladder — run in order, stop when found:
1. EXACT: search_entities by title words and by tags ("second-brain", "daily-note", topic tags). "Yesterday's notes" and "last Tuesday" resolve by deterministic daily-note names — no search needed.
2. GRAPH: notes about a tracked thing → query_relationships on that entity for REFERENCES edges to notes ("what did I note about Grandma?" walks her edges).
3. SEMANTIC: search_lore in natural language for half-remembered content ("that note with the hydration table"), scoped to entity_type "reference" when the world is busy. Results carry full chunk content — quote from a result only when it is the complete chunk; otherwise fetch the chunk itself.

Presentation: for "find my notes on X", list the matches (title, captured date, one-line gist) and offer full text; for "show me the note", the verbatim text IS the answer. Distinguish quoting ("your note says:") from loose recall ("you have a note about that"). If nothing is found, say so and name the nearest misses — never improvise a note's contents.

lore.title: Workflow: Review & Tidy
lore.content:
Triggered by "review my notes", "tidy my notebook", a weekly review habit, or when inbox clutter is genuinely in the way of a task.

Surface, don't act — every change below waits for a yes:
1. Unpromoted captures: daily-note chunks that clearly outgrew the inbox — offer promotion.
2. Untagged and unlinked notes: notes missing topic tags or subject links — propose tags from the Note Index.
3. Near-duplicates: notes whose titles or contents overlap — offer a merge. A merge keeps BOTH texts verbatim: each incoming chunk is copied into the surviving note as "Note (merged from <name>, <captured date>)" (numbered "… 2/3)" per part when multi-part), the incoming note's edges move over, the copies are read back and confirmed byte-identical, and ONLY THEN is the empty shell deleted. Nothing is reworded during a merge.
4. Superseded notes: when the user says a note is out of date, set status: "superseded", add one final lore line naming the successor, and leave the text intact — old notes are history, not garbage.
5. Index drift: Note Index lines whose tags match no note — offer to strike them; topic tags in use but missing from the index — add them.

Cadence: run a review when asked; mention it unprompted only when the daily-note backlog is blocking something. Never nag. Afterwards report what was checked and what changed, honestly, including "nothing needed".

data.short_description: Hub for the Second Brain app. Notes are found by the second-brain tag; this entity holds notebook-wide lore, including the Note Index — the controlled vocabulary of topic tags.

properties.note: App hub managed by the Second Brain app
